6.2- Training Needs Analysis (TNA): 
6.2.1- The assigned L&D Supervisor performs a detailed analysis of the request to confirm 
the performance gap, target audience and define clear. 
6.2.2- The L&D Supervisor consults with relevant Subject Matter Experts (SMEs) and the 
requesting manager to validate the need and scope. 
6.2.3- The L&D Manager coordinates with Human Resources to identify the training 
programs the company needs to implement. 
6.3- Training Design & Development: 
6.3.1- Based on the TNA (code: PS/L&D-F-02) and taking into consideration each 
employee’s individual training plan form (code: PS/L&D-F-03) if applicable, the 
L&D supervisor coordinates with the relevant department manager to determine 
whether the training can be delivered internally by the department. If internal 
delivery is not feasible, the L&D supervisor coordinates with a certified external 
training provider, which will be responsible for developing the training materials 
and delivering the training. 
6.3.2- The L&D supervisor shall evaluate the external training provider in training 
providers evaluation form (code: PS/L&D-F-04). 
6.4- Training Scheduling & Communication: 
6.4.1- The L&D supervisor schedules the training session in coordination with the 
department head to minimize disruption to operational workflow. 
6.4.2- Calendar invitations and communication are sent to all participants and their 
managers with details of the training. It recorded in the training calendar form 
(code: PS/L&D-F-05). 
6.5- Training Delivery: 
6.5.1- The L&D supervisor or an approved trainer delivers the training as planned. 
6.5.2- Attendance is mandatory and recorded on the training attendance record (code: 
PS/L&D-F-06). 
6.5.3- In case of non-attendance, The L&D Supervisor notifies the HR department to take 
the necessary action with the employee.

6.6- Training Evaluation (Level 1 & 2): 
6.6.1- Immediately following the training, participants are required to complete the 
training evaluation form (code: PS/L&D-F-07) to measure satisfaction (Level 1: 
Reaction). 
6.6.2- Learning is assessed through a quiz, practical test, or simulation to confirm 
knowledge transfer (Level 2: Learning). 
6.7- Record Keeping: 
6.7.1- The L&D supervisor collects all training records (attendance, feedback, 
assessments) and files them in the respective employees' training folders, in 
accordance with the documented information procedure. 
6.8- Post-Training Evaluation (Level 3): 
6.8.1- Approximately 30 days after training completion, the L&D supervisor conducts a 
follow-up with the participant's direct manager using the post-training evaluation 
(code: PS/L&D-F-08) to assess the application of skills on the job (Level 3: 
Behavior). 
6.8.2- Based on the manager's feedback, the training is deemed successful, and the loop is 
closed, or a root cause is identified for further action (e.g., refresher training, job aid 
creation), which is initiated by returning to the TNA step (Section 6.2).

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this procedure is to ensure that all medical services rendered are authorized in 
compliance with insurance requirements. This process safeguards against claim denials, 
supports accurate billing, and promotes effective coordination between clinical, 
administrative, and billing departments. It establishes a standardized workflow for verifying 
eligibility, obtaining prior authorizations, monitoring active approvals, and resolving denials 
or expired authorizations. 
2- Scope: 
2.1- This procedure applies to all staff involved in the authorization workflow, including the 
authorization department, client relations team, therapists/clinical staff, and credentialing 
department. 
2.2-It covers all stages of the authorization process — from patient eligibility verification and 
submission of prior authorization requests to monitoring approvals, renewals, and denial 
management — for all patients receiving treatment under commercial, workers’ 
compensation (WC), or no-fault (NF) insurance. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
3.2- External Regulatory and Industry References: 
• HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) 
• ICD-10-CM, CPT, and HCPCS Coding Standards  
• Payer-specific Authorization Guidelines

4- Terms / Terminology: 
4.1- Authorization: 
Official approval from an insurance company confirming that the proposed treatment or 
service is covered under the patient’s plan. 
4.2- Eligibility and Benefits Check: 
The process of verifying a patient’s active insurance coverage and plan benefits. 
4.3- Initial Evaluation: 
The first therapy session during which the patient’s condition is assessed and 
documented. 
4.4- Plan of Care (POC): 
A treatment plan created by the therapist that defines the frequency, duration, and type of 
services to be rendered. 
4.5- Prior Approval: 
A requirement by some payers to obtain authorization before providing certain services. 
4.6- Patient Filtration: 
A daily review process to confirm that all patients who attended the clinic have valid 
authorizations for their sessions. 
4.7- Re-Evaluation: 
A follow-up assessment documenting patient progress and justifying continued care or 
additional sessions. 
4.8- Credentialing Information: 
Provider details (NPI, Tax ID, location) necessary for accurate authorization submission. 
4.9- Medical Necessity Denial: 
A rejection issued by an insurer indicating insufficient justification for treatment based 
on medical evidence. 
4.10- Peer-to-Peer Review: 
A discussion between the provider and an insurance medical reviewer to advocate for 
approval after a denial. 
4.11- EMR System: 
The digital system used for documentation, scheduling, and record management. 
4.12- CPT and ICD-10 Codes: 
Standard codes used to describe procedures and diagnoses for insurance and billing 
purposes.

4.13- Login Sheet: 
An internal document containing each clinic’s specific information, including 
address, Tax ID, and insurance portal login credentials, used for claim 
submissions. 
4.14- Submission Sheet: 
A record sheet that tracks all claim submissions for each client, including 
submission dates, payer names, and claim status. 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
- Responsible for ensuring the processes described in this 
procedure achieve their intended outcomes. 
- Responsible for ensuring that the procedure requirements 
are integrated within business processes. 
Senior Authorization 
Specialist 
- Oversees the authorization process, ensures compliance 
with payer policies, and resolves escalated or urgent 
cases. 
Authorization 
Specialist 
- Conducts daily patient filtration, submits prior 
authorization requests, monitors approvals, manages 
denials, and maintains accurate records in the EMR. 
Client Relations Team 
- Verifies insurance eligibility and provides up-to-date 
patient insurance details to the authorization department. 
Client/Front Desk 
- Provide clinical documentation such as evaluations, 
POCs, and progress notes and schedule history required 
to justify authorizations. 
Credentialing 
Department 
- Ensures providers and locations are in-network with 
insurance plans and resolves credentialing discrepancies.  
Maintains login sheets and credentialing plan updates.

- Coordinates with the authorization department to align 
approved authorizations with submitted claims and 
manage denial corrections.

Page 5 of 8 
6- Procedures: 
6.1- Patient Filtration: 
Each day, the authorization specialist reviews the clinic’s schedule for the previous day 
to ensure that all patients who attended have valid authorizations for services rendered. 
6.1.1- For clinics using WebPT: The list of attended patients is extracted directly from 
WebPT (code: PS/B&A-F-04). Patients requiring authorizations are added to the 
external clients combined authorization sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-07), which 
centralizes all WebPT-based authorization tracking. 
6.1.2- For clinics using other EMR systems: The authorization specialist fills the 
submission sheet - authorization subsheet (code: PS/B&A-F-08) with the patient 
names and dates of service from the previous day. Patients requiring authorization 
are then monitored from the authorization tab within the submission sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-08). This approach is used because non-WebPT systems lack efficient 
authorization tracking tools like Reminder Mate (code: PS/B&A-F-05) and Dr 
Chrono (code: PS/B&A-F-06). 
6.1.3- The specialist then reviews each patient’s authorization status (valid, expired, 
missing, or pending renewal). Any missing or expired authorizations are flagged for 
follow-up and request initiation. 
6.2- Requesting Prior Approvals: 
When authorizations are required, the authorization specialist gathers and submits 
requests based on insurance requirements and clinical documentation. 
6.2.1- Trigger: When a patient’s evaluation or treatment requires prior authorization, or an 
existing authorization is near expiry within the last 24 hours. 
6.2.2- Action: 
o Verify plan requirements for initial evaluations, re-evaluations, or expired 
authorizations through the department reference or insurance communication if 
needed. 
o Gather patient demographics, and clinical notes (evaluation, POC, progress reports) 
from EMR system., credentialing data from login sheet (code: PS/CRE-F-05) 
o Confirm ICD-10 and CPT codes. 
o Submit the request via fax, portal, or phone, depending on payer guidelines.

6.2.3- Output: 
Authorization request successfully submitted with tracking/reference number. 
6.2.4- Communication: 
Updates recorded in the EMR and Authorization/Submission Sheet; relevant 
stakeholders (therapist, billing, client) notified if required. 
6.3- Monitoring and Follow-Up: 
After submission, each authorization request is monitored by the authorization 
specialist daily until a decision is received. 
6.3.1- Review response timeframes (typically 0–10 business days). 
6.3.2- Check portal for status updates. 
6.3.3- For payers without portals, contact via phone after the expected processing window. 
6.3.4- Update EMR and submission/authorization sheet with the latest status (Pending, 
Approved, Denied, Partially Approved). 
6.3.5- Notify therapists and billing of approvals or denials. 
6.4- Managing Denials and Expired Authorizations: 
If an authorization is denied, expired, or partially approved, the authorization specialist 
investigates and resolves the issue promptly. Common scenarios include: 
6.4.1- Active approval with another provider: Submit patient consent (code: PS/B&A-F-
09) or discharge note. 
6.4.2- Plan limitations met: Verify benefit renewal dates. 
6.4.3- Missing documentation: Request and attach missing notes or forms from the 
client/medical auditing team according to the client’s contract. 
6.4.4- Credentialing issues: Coordinate with credentialing department via ClickUp in 
credentialing inquiries form (code: PS/B&A-F-10). 
6.4.5- Medical necessity denial: Initiate a peer-to-peer review if applicable. 
6.4.6- All denial resolutions are recorded in the EMR and submission sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-08). 
6.5- Recordkeeping and Reporting: 
6.5.1- Maintain updated records of all authorizations, pending requests, and resolutions in 
the tracking sheet comments for additional notes. 
6.5.2- Keep copies of evaluation reports, POCs, referrals, and fax confirmations in shared 
folders. 
6.5.3- Coordinate with Billing to ensure alignment between authorizations and submitted 
claims.

1- Purpose: 
To provide a structured process for welcoming and integrating new employees into the 
organization. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure applies to all employees involved in the onboarding and offboarding 
processes across the organization. It covers the activities, roles, and responsibilities 
related to employee joining and separation, including coordination between HR, direct 
managers, IT, and other supporting departments to ensure proper access setup, 
documentation, asset handover, and system deactivation in accordance with company 
policies. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
3.2- labor law 2025 and insurance law no. 148 2019. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Employee Benefits: 
Refers to all non-wage compensations provided to employees, including social insurance, 
medical insurance, allowances, and other approved perks.

4.2-Social Insurance: 
A mandatory governmental system that provides employees with social security coverage 
as per Egyptian Labor Law. 
4.3-Medical Insurance: 
A private health coverage plan provided by the company through an authorized insurance 
provider, activated after the employee successfully completes the probation period. 
4.4- Probation Period: 
The initial employment evaluation period (typically three months) during which the 
employee’s performance and conduct are assessed before confirmation of employment. 
4.5- Allowance: 
A fixed financial benefit (e.g., transportation, meal, or mobile allowance) provided based 
on job grade, position, or company policy. 
4.6- Provider: 
The external company contracted to deliver specific benefits or insurance services to 
employees. 
4.7- HRIS (Human Resources Information System): 
The digital platform used by HR to manage and record employee data, including benefits 
information.  
4.8- Department Manager: 
The manager of the department to which the resigning employee belongs. 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
Responsible for ensuring the processes described in this 
procedure achieve their intended outcomes. 
Responsible for ensuring that the procedure requirements 
are integrated within business processes. 
HR manager  
Oversees process completion 
Senior HR Generalist 
Oversees process completion, orientation, and compliance. 
HR Coordinator 
Handles administrative preparation and documentation. 
IT 
Create the mail and webpt and provide the necessary 
equipment.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Pre-Onboarding (Start Date): 
6.1.1- HR coordinator receives hiring approval and employment offer acceptance via 
Email or WhatsApp. 
6.1.2- Prepare employee file (National ID, Original Birth Certificate, Original Graduation 
Certificate, Military Certificate, Original Police Record, Social Insurance 
Statement, Work Stub, 6 personal photos and medical check template no. 111). 
6.2- Day 1 – Employee Arrival: 
6.2.1- HR coordinator greets the employee and ensures attendance registration. 
6.2.2- Verify and collect all required documents (ID, certificates, social insurance form, 
etc.). 
6.2.3- Issue ID card, email credentials, and orientation schedule in coordination with IT 
department. 
6.2.4- Introduce to the direct manager and team. 
6.2.5- Send welcome email with joining instructions, reporting time, and required 
documents. 
6.2.6- Coordinate with IT for system and email access. 
6.2.7- Give the new hire orientation session.  
6.2.8- The employee signs the contract (code: PS/HR-F-01) and agrees to its terms. 
6.2.9- The employee signs the Form 1 for social insurance enrollment. 
6.3- Orientation and Training: 
6.3.1- HR Coordinator conducts company orientation, covering. 
6.3.2- Company mission, values, and policies. 
6.3.2- Code of conduct and attendance rules. 
6.3.4- ISO induction. 
6.3.5- Payroll, medical, and social insurance overview. 
6.3.6- Departmental induction led by the line manager.

6.4- System and Benefits Registration: 
6.4.1- HR Coordinator inputs new hire data into HR system. 
6.4.2- Senior HR Generalist Register employee in payroll, medical insurance. 
6.4.3- Verify activation of all accounts and benefits. 
6.5- Follow-Up and Feedback: 
6.5.1- HR coordinator checks within one week to ensure all documents are complete. 
Note: This procedure shall be reviewed annually by the HR department or when process 
changes occur. 
6.6- Offboarding Process (Employee Exit Process): 
6.6.1- Initiation of Offboarding: 
6.6.1.1- The offboarding process is initiated when an employee submits a resignation 
form receives a termination decision, reaches the end of their contract, or in 
cases of death or medical incapacity. 
6.6.1.2- Resignation approval is obtained through the line manager’s signature on the 
resignation form, which is then sent via email to HR by the employee or the 
direct manager. 
6.6.1.3- The HR specialist schedule an exit meeting with the employee. 
6.6.1.4- The HR specialist notify relevant departments (IT, Admin, Finance, Payroll, 
Security, etc.) via email. 
6.6.2- Documentation & Compliance: 
6.6.2.1- The HR Specialist collects all required documents, including the resignation 
form or termination decision, clearance and asset return form and exit 
interview form (code: PS/HR-F-02). 
6.6.2.2- The HR Specialist prepares the official forms, including Form 6 (Social 
Insurance Termination Form), updates the employee’s status on HRIS, and 
prepares the final payroll details. 
6.6.2.3- The Department Manager is responsible for ensuring the full handover of tasks 
and pending work, access permissions and files, and project documents.

6.6.2.4- The Department Manager collects all company-owned items from the 
employee, including laptop or mobile, ID card, access cards, keys, and any 
tools or equipment in coordination with IT department. 
6.6.2.5- The employee, the department manager, IT department and HR all sign the 
clearance and assets return form (code: PS/HR-F-03) and submit it to HR. 
6.6.2.6- The IT Department is responsible for disabling access to the employee’s email, 
HR systems, ERP/CRM systems, and internal communication tools, backing 
up essential work files, and confirming the closure of all accounts. 
6.6.3- Clearance & Final Settlement 
6.6.3.1- The HR Specialist processes the final salary, including unpaid salary, leave 
encashment, deductions (loans, penalties, etc.), and benefits settlement. 
6.6.3.2- The HR Specialist issues HR letter (Code: PS/HR-F-04) (if applicable) and the 
clearance and assets return form (code: PS/HR-F-03) receipt signed by the 
employee. 
6.6.3.3- The HR Specialist submits Form 6 to the Social Insurance Authority, removes 
the employee from the medical insurance list, and archives all exit documents 
in the personnel file. 
6.6.3.4- The Senior HR Specialist conducts the exit interview, discussing the reasons 
for leaving and collecting feedback on the work environment, leadership, HR 
processes, and company culture. 
6.6.3.5- The HR Specialist confirms that all relevant departments have signed off on the 
clearance and assets return form (code: PS/HR-F-03). 
6.6.3.6- The employee receives the final settlement, HR letter (Code: PS/HR-F-04) and 
any other required certificates or documents.

1- Purpose: 
The objective of this SOP is to outline the process for managing demands and requests within 
the Service Delivery Department at COB Solution. This includes: 
• Defining the difference between demands (long-term service needs) and requests 
(short-term, specific inquiries).  
• Establishing a structured workflow for handling demands and requests across various 
departments (Billing, Authorization, Credentialing, Medical Auditing, and Customer 
Care). 
• Ensuring timely, accurate, and efficient resolution of inquiries to maintain operational 
efficiency and customer satisfaction 
2- Scope: 
This SOP applies to all activities related to the management of demands and requests within 
the Service Delivery Department at COB Solution. It covers: 
• Receiving, categorizing, and resolving demands and requests. 
• Coordination between internal teams (Billing, Authorization, Credentialing, Medical 
Auditing, and Customer Care). 
• Documentation and communication protocols for all inquiries. 
3- References: 
- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015.

4.6- ClickUp: 
CRM and tracking system used for managing demands, requests, and workflows. 
4.7- QuickBooks: 
A cloud-based accounting software used for managing financial transactions, including 
invoicing, expense tracking, and payroll processing.

- Ensure that all inquiries are categorized and prioritized according 
to urgency and type. 
- Coordinate with relevant departments (Billing, Authorization, 
Credentialing, Medical Auditing, and Customer Care) to resolve 
inquiries. 
- Monitor the progress of inquiries and ensure timely resolution in 
accordance with SLAs. 
- Document all actions and communications related to demands 
and requests. 
- Act as the primary point of contact for clients and internal teams 
regarding the status of inquiries. 
- Provide regular updates to stakeholders on the status of demands 
and requests.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Process for Demands and Requests in Billing and Authorization: 
6.1.1- Receiving Inquiries from Clients: 
• SD receives the request via email or calls. 
• Review the inquiry and clarify details if needed. 
• Categorize the inquiry (e.g., system issues, reimbursement, procedure updates). 
• Direct the inquiry to the relevant internal team or contact the client for resolution. 
• Document all actions and communicate the resolution to the client and internal 
team on ClickUp. 
6.1.2- Receiving Inquiries from the Billing and Authorization Team: 
• Review the inquiry and gather additional details if necessary. 
• Categorize the inquiry (e.g., billing discrepancies, authorization issues). 
• Determine the action (internal clarification or client contact). 
• Resolve the inquiry and document all steps taken.

6.2- Process for Demands and Requests in Credentialing: 
6.2.1- Receiving and Tracking Requests: 
• Log credentialing requests (new enrollments, re-credentialing, provider updates). 
• Validate and categorize requests, flagging urgent cases for priority handling. 
6.2.2- Monitoring Progress: 
• Follow up with the Credentialing Team to check the status of pending applications. 
• Escalate delays and ensure compliance with payer requirements. 
6.2.3- Resolving Issues: 
• Verify completion of credentialing and update all systems. 
• Notify stakeholders and close the request 
6.3- Process for Demands and Requests in Medical Auditing 
6.3.1-Receiving and Logging Audit Requests from Clients: 
• The Account Manager receives the inquiry directly from the client via email or call 
(provider, clinic, or designated contact). 
• The Account Manager reviews the inquiry, confirms the details, and logs the request 
in ClickUp. 
• The Account Manager assigns the request to the Medical Auditing Team for  
assessment and action. 
6.3.2- Documentation Review & Report Preparation (Medical Auditing Team): 
• Once assigned, the Medical Auditing Team reviews all relevant medical records,  
coding entries, and documentation. 
• The team identifies all documentation gaps, coding issues, and compliance  
discrepancies. 
• The Medical Auditing Team is fully responsible for preparing, finalizing, and issuing 
the complete audit report, including: 
o Findings 
o Required corrections 
o Root causes or patterns 
o Preventive and corrective action recommendations 
• The finalized report is shared with providers, the Billing Team, and relevant 
stakeholders.

6.3.3- Resolution & Closure : 
• The Account Manager coordinates with providers and billing staff to ensure all 
recommended corrections are completed. 
• The Medical Auditing Team verifies the corrections if needed. 
• The Account Manager updates the inquiry status in ClickUp and closes the request 
once resolved. 
6.4- Process for Demands and Requests in Customer Care: 
6.4.1- Receiving and Logging Customer Care Inquiries 
• The Account Manager receives the inquiry directly from the client (clinic, provider, 
or designated representative). 
• The Account Manager reviews the inquiry, clarifies missing details, and logs the 
request in ClickUp. 
• The Account Manager assigns the request to the Customer Care Team for handling. 
6.4.2- Handling and Resolving Customer Care Inquiries (Customer Care Team) : 
• Once assigned, the Customer Care Team reviews the request and validates details in 
the EMR or other systems. 
• The Customer Care Team is fully responsible for resolving all patient-related 
operational inquiries, including: 
o Appointment scheduling or confirmation 
o Patient communication follow-ups 
o EMR navigation or front-desk workflow support 
• The Customer Care Team documents all actions taken and updates the inquiry status 
in ClickUp. 
• If additional clarification or clinic action is required, the team coordinates 
accordingly. 
6.4.3- Resolution & Closure: 
• The Customer Care Team notifies the Account Manager once action is completed. 
• The Account Manager reviews and confirms that the inquiry has been fully resolved. 
• The Account Manager closes the request in ClickUp and updates the client if  
applicable.

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this document is to provide a clear and detailed procedure for submitting 
medical claims to insurance companies. It outlines the steps necessary to ensure accurate and 
timely submission of claims. 
2- Scope: 
2.1- This procedure applies to all administrative and billing staff, medical coders, and insurance 
claim processors responsible for preparing and submitting medical claims on behalf of the 
practice or facility. It defines the boundaries of claim submission activities, ensuring 
consistency and compliance across all billing operations. 
2.2- The procedure covers the entire process of claim submission — from gathering and 
verifying patient information to submitting claims through the appropriate electronic 
platforms (such as Therabill, Webpt billing Jopari, etc.). It ensures that all required 
documentation, authorizations, and coding modifiers are accurately included. This 
procedure applies to all types of claims, including Commercial Insurance, Workers’ 
Compensation (WC), and No-Fault (NF) claims.

3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
3.2- External Regulatory and Industry References: 
● HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) — Compliance for secure 
handling of patient health and billing information. 
● CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) Guidelines — For claims submission 
requirements, billing codes, and modifier usage. 
● ICD-10-CM, CPT, and HCPCS Coding Standards — Official medical coding systems 
required for claim accuracy. 
● Payer-specific Billing Guidelines — Including Commercial Insurance, Workers’ 
Compensation (WC), and No-Fault (NF) carrier rules. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- EMR (Electronic Medical Record): 
A digital version of a patient’s medical chart containing clinical and billing information 
used for claim generation and tracking. 
4.2- Practice Mate: 
The practice management and billing system used to create, process, and submit claims 
electronically to the clearinghouse or payer. 
4.3- CPT Codes (Current Procedural Terminology): 
Standardized codes used to describe medical, surgical, and diagnostic services for billing 
and insurance claim purposes. 
4.4- ICD-10 Codes (International Classification of Diseases, 10th Revision): 
Codes used to classify and report medical diagnoses and reasons for patient visits in claims 
processing. 
4.5- CMS-1500 Form: 
The standard paper claim form used by healthcare providers to bill insurance carriers for 
services rendered. 
4.6- UN (Unauthorized): 
Indicates that a claim or service was rendered without the required authorization or pre-
approval from the insurance payer.

4.7- Missing (Missing Note): 
Refers to the absence of required clinical documentation or notes needed to support the 
claim submission. 
4.8- Clinical Note: 
A detailed record written by the healthcare provider that documents the patient’s encounter, 
treatment, and progress. It serves as the primary source of medical justification for billing 
and claim submission. 
4.9- Date of Service (DOS): 
The specific date on which a healthcare service was provided to the patient. 
4.10- Modifiers: 
Two-digit codes added to CPT or HCPCS codes to provide additional information about 
the service performed, without changing its definition. 
4.11- Login Sheet: 
An internal document containing each clinic’s specific information, including address, Tax 
ID, and insurance portal login credentials, used for claim submissions. 
4.12- Credentialing Plan: 
A document containing the client’s credentialing information with various insurance 
payers, ensuring accurate setup and claim routing. 
4.13- Submission Sheet: 
A record sheet that tracks all claim submissions for each client, including submission dates, 
payer names, and claim status. 
4.14- Clearinghouse: 
A third-party intermediary that electronically transmits healthcare claims from the 
provider’s billing system to the insurance payer. It validates claim data, ensures 
compliance with payer requirements, and returns acceptance or rejection reports. 
4.15- Clearinghouse Rejection: 
An error response generated by the clearinghouse when a submitted claim fails initial 
validation checks due to missing or incorrect data. 
4.16- Commercial Insurance: 
A type of health insurance provided by private companies that covers medical services for 
policyholders. Claims under commercial insurance must include accurate policy and 
authorization details as per payer requirements.

4.17- No-Fault Insurance (NF): 
Insurance coverage that pays for medical treatment and related expenses after an accident, 
regardless of who was at fault. Claims under No-Fault require accident details, Assignment 
of Benefits (AOB) forms, and referral documentation. 
4.18- Workers’ Compensation Insurance (WC): 
Insurance that provides coverage for employees injured on the job, including medical 
treatment and wage replacement. WC claims must include accident information, body site 
details, and a valid date of injury. 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
- Responsible for ensuring the processes described in this 
procedure achieve their intended outcomes. 
- Responsible for ensuring that the procedure requirements are 
integrated within business processes. 
Claim Submission 
Team  
- Responsible for submitting claims to insurance companies on 
behalf of the practice or facility. This includes gathering 
accurate patient information, reviewing CPT codes and 
modifiers, and ensuring proper documentation is attached to the 
claims. 
Credentialing 
Department 
- Ensures that the provider and location are appropriate for the 
specific insurance plan being submitted. They also handle the 
enrollment of each provider with the relevant insurance 
companies to ensure eligibility and proper claim submission. 
Authorization 
Department 
- Responsible for obtaining authorization for each date of service 
when required. They ensure that proper authorization is secured 
Medical Auditing 
Department 
- Ensures the accuracy and completeness of medical 
documentation, including progress notes. They are responsible 
for confirming that all required notes are included, finalized, 
and properly closed after the provider has completed their part.

Page 6 of 10 
6- Procedures: 
6.1- Filling the Submission Records: 
The claim submission process begins with the preparation of the clinic submission 
sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-12), which serves as the primary tracker for all claims. This 
step starts the day after the Date of Service (DOS), once all appointments for that 
date have been finalized in the schedule. The Billing Specialist opens the Submission 
Sheet and enters each patient’s name, date of service. 
6.1.1- Access the clinic Submission Sheet from the COB Account Data Sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-03). 
6.1.2- Cross-check the appointment schedule to ensure all encounters from the DOS are 
captured. 
6.1.3- Record the patient’s name, Date of Service. 
6.1.4- Highlight or tag any encounters missing documentation, unauthorized sessions. 
Note: For clinics utilizing any EMR different than WEBPT, filling the clinic submission 
sheet is the responsibility of the authorization agent handling the client, please refer 
to the authorization procedure. This ensures all encounters are accounted for before 
billing begins, minimizing missed or delayed submissions. 
Output: Complete therapy appointments records of the previous day in the clinic 
submission sheet for internal review 
6.2- Prepare Patient Information: 
Once the clinic submission sheet is ready, the Billing Specialist verifies the accuracy 
of all patient and provider details before initiating claim creation.  
6.2.1- Open the patient’s chart in the EMR system (e.g., Webpt) (code: PS/B&A-F-11) 
6.2.2- Confirm patient identifiers — first name, last name, date of birth, insurance ID, 
Address— match the chart and eligibility record. 
6.2.3- Verify that the referring provider is listed on the chart if needed (According to 
payer’s specific rules). 
6.2.4- Confirm the rendering provider’s credentialing status using the credentialing plan 
sheet (code: PS/CRE-F-09) and Login Sheet (code: PS/CRE-F-05).  
6.2.5- Correct any discrepancies in the above by coordinating with client relations or the 
clinic Google space. 
Ensuring all data is correct at this stage avoids claim rejections due to invalid 
demographic or provider information.

Page 7 of 10 
6.3- Verify Insurance Information: 
The Billing Specialist confirms payer details after confirming patient’s info to ensure that 
each claim follows the correct submission route and meets payer-specific requirements. 
6.3.1- Identify the insurance type (Commercial, Workers’ Compensation, or No-Fault) 
from the patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11). 
6.3.2- For Commercial Insurance, confirm eligibility and policy details as provided by 
Client Relations team through the google space. 
6.3.3- For Workers’ Compensation, verify the date of injury and body site against medical 
records. 
6.3.4- For No-Fault Insurance, confirm the date of accident and ensure accident details are 
listed in the CMS-1500 form. 
6.3.5- Note any missing data and communicate it to Client Relations for correction. 
Accurate insurance verification ensures the claim is routed properly to the payer and avoids 
processing delays. 
6.4- Review CPT Codes and Modifiers: 
After confirming insurance details, the Billing Specialist reviews all clinical 
documentation to assign correct CPT codes and necessary modifiers. 
6.4.1- Access progress or medical notes for the patient in the EMR system (code: PS/B&A-
F-13). 
6.4.2- Identify and match CPT codes to documented services in Medical Note form (code: 
PS/B&A-F-13). 
6.4.3- Review ICD-10 codes to ensure diagnosis alignment with the procedure performed. 
6.4.4- Report any missing information to the client or the auditing team according to the 
client’s contract. 
This ensures clinical accuracy and compliance with payer rules, reducing denial risk. 
6.5- Fill Out the CMS-1500 Form: 
Once the claim has been coded and verified, the Billing Specialist completes the 
CMS-1500 form in Practice Mate or the designated software. 
6.5.1- Open the CMS-1500 template and enter patient demographics, insurance details, and 
provider information. 
6.5.2- Input the Date of Service, CPT codes, ICD-10 codes, and modifiers. 
6.5.3- Include the referring provider (if applicable), authorization number (if applicable), 
and accident details for WC/NF claims. 
6.5.4- Review each field for completeness and accuracy. 
Accurate completion of the CMS-1500 form ensures the claim passes clearinghouse 
and payer validation.

Page 8 of 10 
6.6- Submit the Claim: 
Once the claim has been reviewed and approved, the Claim Submission Specialist 
proceeds to finalize and transmit it through the billing system. Using the designated 
submission platform, the specialist selects the completed claim and presses the 
“Transmit Claim” button to submit it electronically to the clearinghouse or payer. 
After submission, the specialist immediately updates the clinic submission sheet 
(code: PS/B&A-F-12) with the submission date. 
This ensures that all transmitted claims are accurately logged and traceable for 
follow-up, reconciliation, and reporting. 
6.6.1: Open the patient’s claim within the applicable billing software. 
6.6.2: Review one final time for accuracy and ensure all required attachments (referral or 
authorization) are included if applicable. 
6.6.3: Press the “Transmit Claim” button to send the claim electronically to the 
clearinghouse or payer. 
6.6.4: Once transmitted, record the submission date and the submitting agent code in the 
clinic submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-12). 
This simplified process ensures quick, accurate, and traceable claim submission with 
minimal manual intervention while maintaining proper documentation control. 
6.7- Monitoring and Updating Submission Sheet: 
After submission, the Billing Specialist monitors the claim status and updates the 
clinic submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-12) to reflect current progress. 
6.7.1- Access the transmits claim dashboard (code: PS/B&A-F-14) to check the submission 
status of each claim.  
6.7.2- Review clearinghouse responses daily for any rejections or errors. 
6.7.3- For rejected claims, identify the reason (e.g., invalid payer ID, missing modifier, 
incomplete information) in Rejection Reason Panel Form (code: PS/B&A-F-15). 
6.7.4- Correct the issue in Practice Mate, resubmit the claim, and update the clinic 
submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-12) with the new submission date. 
Note: The interface and steps for filling out the CMS-1500 form and transmitting the claim 
may vary slightly depending on the version or configuration of Practice Mate or the 
billing platform used. However, all systems follow the same standard procedure 
outlined above — completing the claim form accurately, verifying all required 
details, and transmitting the claim for payer processing.

1- Purpose: 
The objective of this procedure is to establish a clear, consistent, and structured approach to 
the recruitment and selection process within the organization. It aims to ensure the timely 
acquisition of qualified and competent candidates in alignment with the company’s strategic 
workforce needs, while promoting fairness, transparency, and compliance with legal and 
organizational standards. 
2- Scope: 
2.1- This procedure applies to all recruitment and selection activities conducted within the 
organization, covering full-time, part-time, temporary, and contractual positions across 
all departments and business units. 
2.2- The scope includes the end-to-end hiring process—from manpower planning and job 
requisition, through sourcing and interviewing, to final selection, offer issuance, and 
onboarding coordination. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
3.2- labor law 2025 and insurance law no. 148 2019 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- SOP: Standard Operating Procedure 
4.2- SOP Owner: The author of the SOP 
4.3- QA: Quality Assurance

5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
HR Manager 
The role is responsible for developing and executing recruitment strategies 
aligned with organizational goals, leading and mentoring the recruitment 
team, and collaborating with department heads to identify staffing needs 
and define job requirements. It oversees candidate sourcing through 
multiple channels, tracks and reports key recruitment metrics, ensures 
compliance with legal, organizational, and diversity standards, manages 
external recruitment partners when needed, approves job offers and salary 
packages, and ensures all candidate documentation is properly collected 
and organized. 
Talent 
Acquisition 
Specialist 
The role is responsible for drafting and publishing job advertisements in 
coordination with hiring managers, sourcing and screening candidates 
through various platforms, conducting initial interviews and assessments, 
and maintaining accurate records in the ATS. It also coordinates interview 
scheduling and feedback, participates in candidate evaluations, provides 
timely feedback to the TA team, collaborates with HR on final hiring 
decisions, and supports the job offer and onboarding processes. 
HR 
coordinator  
The role supports the recruitment process by assisting in posting job 
advertisements, 
scheduling 
interviews, 
and 
communicating 
with 
candidates. It helps prepare job offers, manages accepted applicants, and 
assists with the onboarding process, including coordinating equipment and 
system access.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Workforce Planning & Job Requisition: 
6.1.1- Department head identifies the need for a new hire or replacement. 
6.1.2- Hiring manager requests hiring via email. The email must include job title, 
department, salary range, and justification.  
6.1.3- HR manager reviews and approves the requisition via email. 
6.1.4- HR manager responsible for doing job analysis (code: PS/HR-F-05) for each job.  
6.2- Job Description Preparation: 
6.2.1- Talent acquisition specialist coordinates with the hiring manager to draft or update 
the job description (code: PS/HR-F-06). 
6.2.2- JD includes job title, responsibilities, qualifications, skills, and reporting structure. 
6.3- Job Posting: 
6.3.1- The TA specialist posts the job on internal and external platforms (e.g., Internal (Via 
Email), LinkedIn, Wuzzuf, social media etc.). 
6.3.2- Job is also shared via employee referral programs or recruitment agencies, if 
applicable. 
6.4- Sourcing & Screening: 
6.4.1- TA Specialist actively sources candidates using job boards, ATS, and professional 
networks. 
6.4.2- Applications are screened based on JD criteria. 
6.4.3- Shortlisted candidates are contacted for an initial phone or video screening. 
6.5- Interview Process: 
6.5.1- Upon arrival, the interviewee fills out the application form (code: PS/HR-F-07). 
6.5.2- First-round interviews conducted by TA. 
6.5.3- Second-round technical/managerial interviews conducted by the hiring manager 
and/or relevant team leads. 
6.5.4- Final interviews may include upper management or panel interviews depending on 
the position level.

Employment may be terminated by either party giving two months’ notice. 
Employment of 
Relatives 
In order to maintain the integrity of the internal control and management systems, the 
company shall not allow the employment of “closely related” persons in the same 
department. “Closely related” in this context will include spouses, parents, children, siblings, 
in-laws, cousins, nieces, nephews, uncles and aunts. In the event of a change in the relationship 
between present employees, the persons involved must inform the Human Capital 
immediately. 
Confidentiality 
You shall not without the consent of the employer disclose to any third party any information 
concerning neither the business of the employer nor the contents of employment contract. 
Offer Validity 
This job offer is valid for five days from the date of being corresponded to you. Should you not 
confirm your acceptance within the validity period; the offer will automatically become 
invalid. 
Applicable Laws 
and Jurisdiction 
This Employment Offer is governed by the Egyptian Labor Law and any dispute or claim 
arising out of or relating to this employment Offer, shall exclusively be settled by the 
competent courts in Egypt without reference to conflict of laws and rules. 
 
                                                          
Human Resources Manager 
 
 
Haitham Zayed 
 
Managing Director 
I hereby accept your job offer

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this policy is to establish a structured and transparent escalation process that 
ensures timely and effective resolution of issues, incidents, and concerns across all COB 
departments. This policy aims to enhance accountability, support decision-making, and foster 
a culture of open and responsible communication. 
2- Scope: 
This escalation policy applies to all COB employees, across all locations and departments, 
including full-time, part-time, temporary, and outsourced staff. The policy ensures that all 
concerns, conflicts, operational issues, or misconduct are handled through a structured and 
hierarchical communication process to promote accountability, transparency, and timely 
resolution. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
3.2- labor law 2025 and insurance law no. 148 2019 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Escalation:  
Refers to the process of raising an issue, concern, or incident to a higher authority or 
management level when: 
• The matter cannot be resolved at the current level. 
• The issue exceeds the employee’s authority or scope. 
• The situation is urgent, time-sensitive, or has potential business/operational impact.

6.2- Types of Issues Covered: 
6.2.1- Interpersonal & Conduct Issues: 
- Conflicts between colleagues.  
- Disrespectful or unprofessional behavior. 
- Communication breakdowns. 
- Harassment or discrimination allegations. 
6.2.2- Operational & Administrative Issues: 
- Process delays or recurring workflow bottlenecks. 
- Misalignment in interdepartmental coordination.  
- Mismanagement of duties or recurring errors. 
- System or IT related failures impacting daily operations. 
6.2.3- Compliance & Risk-Related Issues: 
- Breach of policy or procedures.  
- Data privacy or confidentiality concerns.  
- Regulatory or licensing risks. 
- Patient/client safety and service quality concerns. 
6.2.4- HR and Employment Issues: 
- Attendance, time-off, or scheduling violations.  
- Concerns with managerial conduct or favoritism.  
- Payroll or compensation disputes.  
- Performance appraisal disagreements.

6.2.5- Escalation of Customer-Facing Issues: 
- Unresolved or recurring customer complaints.  
- Service failure with reputational impact.  
- Improper handling of sensitive or escalated client cases. 
6.2.6- Who Can Initiate an Escalation: 
- Any employee who is directly affected by or aware of an unresolved issue.  
- Team leaders or supervisors who identify patterns or critical incidents.  
- Managers when subordinate resolution attempts have failed.  
- HR when policy, conduct, or legal risks are identified. 
6.3- Guidelines for Escalation: 
6.3.1- Attempt resolution at the lowest level possible before escalating. 
6.3.2- Use official channels: email, escalation register form (code: PS/HR-F-09), or ticketing 
systems (if applicable). 
6.3.3- Be factual and objective in communication. 
6.3.4- Always maintain confidentiality and professionalism. 
6.3.5- Follow the hierarchy unless safety, legal, or ethical concerns require direct higher-
level action. 
6.3.6- Keep involved parties informed about the status of the issue. 
6.4- Misuse of Escalation: 
6.4.1- Skipping levels without valid justification.  
6.4.2- Submitting unfounded or exaggerated claims.  
6.4.3- Using escalation to harass or pressure colleagues or leadership.

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this procedure is to define a standardized procedure for receiving, validating, 
and releasing patient medical records in accordance with privacy regulations and 
organizational policies. It aims to ensure that all medical record requests are processed 
securely, accurately, and in a timely manner. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure applies to the process of: 
• Receiving medical record requests 
• Validating HIPAA authorizations 
• Retrieving patient documentation 
• Releasing records securely 
3- References: 
3.1- Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) Privacy Rule: 
Ensures compliance with regulations governing the secure handling and protection of 
patient health and billing information. 
3.2- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015.

4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- [HIPAA Release Form]: 
A signed authorization that permits the release of a patient’s medical records. 
4.2- Itemized Bill: 
A detailed invoice that lists each individual service, procedure, medication, or supply 
provided to a patient, along with the corresponding charges foreach item. 
4.3- Balance Statement: 
A balance statement is a financial summary provided by a healthcare provider, insurance 
company, or organization that lists all charges, payments, adjustments, and remaining 
balances for a patient 
4.4- SOAP Notes: 
A method of documenting patient information using four categories: Subjective, 
Objective, Assessment, and Plan. It is a widely used format for medical documentation. 
Subjective: The patient's reported symptoms, history, and experiences. 
Objective: Measurable data such as physical exams, test results, and observations. 
Assessment: The healthcare provider’s analysis of the situation, including diagnoses. 
Plan: The treatment or actions to be taken, including therapy goals and interventions.

achieve their intended outcomes.

integrated within business processes. 
Medical Records 
Staff 
Receive, verify, and process medical record requests. 
Ensure that all releases are supported by valid HIPAA authorization 
forms.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Receive Request: 
Incoming requests via email, fax, or phone are relayed to the Medical Records Staff. 
When a request is received, the staff logs it in the medical records requests sheet  
(code: PS/B&A-F-16), including requester name, patient details, and requested 
documents. This creates a verifiable record and initiates the processing workflow. 
6.2- Verify HIPAA Release Form: 
Upon logging the request, the staff checks whether a valid HIPAA Release Form is 
attached or previously filed. If unavailable, the requester is contacted and asked to 
provide a signed authorization. Processing does not continue until a compliant form is 
received. Once validated, the authorization is archived for future reference. 
6.3- Retrieve Medical Records: 
With a valid authorization on file, the medical records staff retrieves the necessary 
documents from EMR System. Examples include SOAP notes, itemized bills, balance 
statements, or progress reports. Retrieved documents are reviewed for accuracy before 
release. 
6.4- Send Records to Requester: 
        Completed document sets are released through the requester’s preferred method email or 
fax. Transmission details (date, method, destination) are logged in the Medical Records 
Request Sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-16).

1- Purpose: 
This document describes the non-AV report that determines which patients are confirmed for 
tomorrow's schedule, which patients can be scheduled for a future date, and which patients 
cannot be seen. This report is communicated to customer care, or clients, or both. 
2- Scope: 
The procedure governs the entire daily process of generating, validating and distributing 
patient scheduling eligibility process, which is used by the customer care or clients when the 
patient is being scheduled to verify eligibility. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology: 
4.1- AV:  
It means that a patient has approved visits. 
4.2- Non-AV:  It means that the patient doesn’t have approved visits. 
4.3- On Hold:  It means that we can’t see the patient till the hold reason is resolved. 
4.4- On Hold Can Be Seen: 
  
 
It means the patient is on hold but can be seen as the reason is resolved.

It means that we can’t see the patient as there is an issue with the authorization. 
4.6- Non-AV Can Be Seen: 
  
It means that we can see the patient with no authorization. 
4.7- BV: Benefits Verification. 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
Responsible for ensuring the processes described in this 
procedure achieve their intended outcomes. 
Responsible for ensuring that the procedure requirements 
are integrated within business processes. 
Authorization Specialist

patient authorization status (AV/non-AV), managing 
pending and hold cases, updating workload and tracker 
sheets, reviewing non-AV cases, escalating discrepancies 
to relevant teams, and delivering finalized reports to 
customer care. 
B&A Manager 
Responsible for overseeing the authorization and benefits 
verification process, ensuring accuracy and timeliness of 
reports, reviewing escalated discrepancies from the team, 
approving final decisions on non-AV cases, coordinating 
between authorization, benefits, referrals, and medical 
auditing teams, and ensuring all reports are delivered to 
customer care in accordance with this procedure.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Gathering Information: 
6.1.1- Downloading Authorization Report (code: PS/B&A-F-17) since today till the end of 
the year from WebPT. 
6.1.2- Downloading benefits verification report (code: PS/B&A-F-18) for the next day if 
we are generating (2nd,3rd, or 4th report), and generating report for the day after 
tomorrow if we are generating the 1st report from WebPT 
6.1.3- Collecting information about pending cases and pending cases (code: PS/B&A-F-
19) sheet of PTOC Authorization sheet. 
6.1.4- Collecting information about patients on hold from hold system sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-20). 
6.2- Data Preparation 
6.2.1- Sort Authorization Report Sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-17) by authorization start date in 
descending order and filter only active authorizations. 
6.2.2- Filter benefits verification report (code: PS/B&A-F-18) choose visit status that is 
not equal to no-show or cancelled, and appointment type that is not in this list 
(blocked schedule, consultation, school INDV, group session, El multiple services, 
El single service, center INDV, teletherapy, multiple DOE, provider out, Madison 
square, program eval). 
6.2.3- From workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21) we filter out all request statuses except 
appointment [Not Invoiced, On Appeal / P2P, Denied, Cancelled Session, Wrong 
Appointment Type, Duplicate, and Patient Discharged]. 
6.2.4- From pending cases sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-19), we filter out pending cases only 
from request status. 
6.3- Data Modification and Transformation 
6.3.1- If we are working on 2nd, 3rd, or 4th report, we match patients from this BV 
generated and prepared report and previous BV report to gather patients added on 
the system between both reports generations, we filter them out in workload sheet 
(code: PS/B&A-F-21) to work on them. If we are working on 1st report, we add the 
BV generated report after preparation as it is.

6.3.2- In BV report sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-18), we add 4 columns representing 
authorization, pending status, hold reason, hold description, and hold status.  
6.3.3- For authorization column we match each record in BV sheet with workload sheet 
(code: PS/B&A-F-21) to know whether the patient has active authorization or not, 
if active we add it as AV, otherwise non-AV. 
6.3.4- For pending column, we match pending cases from workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-
F-21) with each record in BV to verify whether the authorization is pending, has an 
issue or not. 
6.3.5- For hold sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-20) we match between each record in BV sheet 
and hold sheet to find out the patient hold reasons, description and status. 
6.3.6- Filter outpatients with “hold” status including (restricted clearance, hold, or closed) 
and move them into hold sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-20).  
6.3.7- Lookup patients with invalid hold reasons and filter them out in “On Hold Can Be 
Seen” tab, the invalid hold reasons can be ranging from: 
• Patients on hold for new case and already got the new case. 
• Patients on hold for pending authorization, while there is an active authorization on 
the system 
• Patients on hold for coverage issue and the current insurance is active with no 
credentialing or policy issues 
• Patients on hold for missing referral, while there is referral attached among the 
documents on patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11). 
6.3.8- Filter out the patients who are not on hold for any reason, with active authorization, 
and move them to workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). Moreover, from non-AV 
and cleared patients, select patients with insurances not requiring authorization and 
append them to workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). Add column to check 
whether the authorization is on the correct case or not. 
6.3.9- Filter out all patients with non-AV status and move them into workload sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-21) 
6.3.10- Filter out all patients with initial examination and new cases from non-AV sheet 
and move them to workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21).

6.3.11- After filtering IE, new cases and non-AV not requiring authorization, filter out 
Non-AV with “Not pending” status and create a copy of them in need justification 
sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-22). 
6.3.12- Open each patient on WebPT from need justification sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-22) 
and start identifying whether they can be seen for the next session and the session 
after it or not. 
• If the patient has active authorization, add it as approved. 
• If the patient authorization expired, add it as expired authorization. 
• If the patient authorization is still pending, add it as pending authorization. 
• If the patient authorization will be initiated tomorrow, add it as authorization in 
process. 
• If the patient has coverage issue add it as coverage issue. 
•  If the patient’s referral is messing add it as missing referral. 
•  If new case is required add it as new case is required. 
6.3.13- Collect justifications and add them to the report sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-23) and 
start matching between non-AV in workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21) records 
and justification to merge them. Once merged: 
• Filter outpatients with authorization in process and expired authorization and 
correctly scheduled add them to Non-AV Can Be Seen column in workload 
sheet. 
• Filter out approved authorizations and move them to AV column in workload 
sheet.

inside this sheet follows the current criteria:  
• Patient can come up to 3 sessions. 
• If the patient has 1 remaining visit in the authorization, we can’t schedule the 
patient until auth is secured.

1- Purpose: 
This procedure defines the standardized process for accurately receiving, reviewing, posting, 
reconciling, and reporting all payments insurance, patient, and third-party ensuring financial 
accuracy, compliance, and transparency across billing operations. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure applies to the billing team responsible for processing payments in the 
organization. It covers all payment types such as insurance payments (Check, ERA, EFT), 
patient payments (Cash, Check), third-party payments, payments requiring portal verification. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Insurance Payments: 
Payments made by an insurance company to the healthcare provider for services rendered 
to a patient. These may be in the form of checks, Electronic Funds Transfers (EFT), or wire 
transfers. 
4.2- Patient Payments: 
Payments made by patients for their portion of healthcare costs, such as copayments, 
deductibles, and coinsurance. These can be made using various methods like credit/debit 
cards, cash, or checks.

Page 3 of 8 
4.3- Explanation of Benefits (EOB): 
A document provided by an insurance company that explains how a claim was processed, 
including the amount covered, patient responsibility (e.g., co-pays), and reasons for denials 
or adjustments. 
4.4- Electronic Remittance Advice (ERA): 
An electronic version of the Explanation of Benefits (EOB) that is sent to the healthcare 
provider, detailing payment processing information, including amounts paid, adjustments, 
or denials. 
4.5- Portal Posting: 
The process of manually logging into an insurance provider's portal to verify and post 
payments if an Explanation of Benefits (EOB) has not been received or uploaded by the 
client. 
4.6- Patient Account: 
A record in the billing system that contains all financial information related to a specific 
patient, including services rendered, payments made, and outstanding balances. 
4.7- Eligibility Sheets: 
A tool used by billing teams to track and record payments for patients treated on a specific 
date, ensuring that payments are posted accurately. 
4.8- Overpayment: 
A situation where the payment received exceeds the amount owed by the patient or 
insurance company for a service. 
4.9- Electronic Funds Transfer (EFT): 
An electronic method of transferring payments directly from the payer’s bank account to 
the healthcare provider’s bank account. EFT ensures faster, more secure, and automated 
receipt of payments compared to paper checks.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Receiving Payments: 
6.1.1- The payment posting process begins when payments are received from insurance 
companies, patients, or third-party sources. The billing supervisor oversees the 
receipt and documentation of all incoming funds. Insurance payments may arrive 
by check, Electronic Funds Transfer (EFT) (code: PS/B&A-F-25), or wire transfer. 
Patient payments can be received through credit or debit cards, cash, or checks, and 
must be accurately recorded in patient’s payment on the EMR system (e.g., WebPT) 
(code: PS/B&A-F-26). 
6.1.2- For PT of the City exclusively, any payment—regardless of source—must first be 
confirmed with the Finance Department before posting. Confirmation is verified 
through the Transaction Sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-27), which serves as the official 
record of payments cleared or deposited. No payment for PT of the City is to be 
posted in the system until Finance verification is completed. This control prevents 
duplication, ensures accuracy, and maintains alignment between Finance and 
Billing. 
6.1.3- Once verified and confirmed, each payment is entered into the billing system and 
corresponding Tracker sheets (code: PS/B&A-F-24), accessible to the billing team 
and department manager. This ensures every payment source is captured and that 
all cash inflows are accurately recorded for complete financial transparency. 
6.2- Reviewing Payment Documents: 
6.2.1- After payments are received, the payment posting specialist reviews all supporting 
documentation, including Explanation of Benefits (EOBs) and Electronic 
Remittance Advices (ERAs). Each document is checked to confirm patient details 
(name, policy number, etc.), payment amount, and any deductions such as co-pays, 
deductibles, or coinsurance. Adjustments, including write-offs or contractual 
reductions, are verified for accuracy. If inconsistencies are found, they are promptly 
flagged and reported to the team leader for clarification. This review step prevents 
posting errors and ensures accurate patient account balances.

Page 6 of 8 
6.3- Gathering Payments: 
6.3.1- Once documentation is verified, the billing team consolidates all payment data to 
ensure that each transaction matches the appropriate claim. For clients with ERA 
integration (e.g., using “Therabill” software), payments are automatically imported 
as ERA files. For check payments, the EOB accompanying each check is collected 
and verified. When clients upload EOBs to the designated Google Drive folder, the 
Billing Team retrieves them for review. 
6.3.2- If an EOB has not been received, the team performs portal posting by logging into 
the payer’s portal to confirm payment issuance. Once verified, the payment details 
are recorded in the eligibility sheets (code: PS/B&A-F-01) corresponding to the date 
of service. This ensures that all payment data —electronic, manual, or portal-
verified— are centralized before posting. 
6.4- Entering Payment Information into the System: 
6.4.1- After confirming payment details, the billing specialist posts each payment to the 
correct patient account within the billing system or designated eligibility sheets 
(code: PS/B&A-F-01) The specialist locates the correct record using the patient’s 
account number, insurance details, or date of birth. For patients treated at multiple 
locations, the DOB is used to confirm the correct site. 
6.4.2- The specialist then locates the appropriate service date, allocates the payment, and 
updates the claim status as follows: 
• Paid: Claim paid in full by insurance. 
• Pending: Claim under insurance processing. 
• Deductible: Claim processed but payment is patient’s responsibility. 
• Denied: Claim rejected; requires denial-management review. 
6.4.3- For denials, the reason from the EOB is entered in the “details” column of the 
eligibility sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-01) Once posted, the updated sheet is visible to 
the Billing Team, Client Relations, and Department Manager. This step guarantees 
accurate allocation of funds and enables timely follow-up on denials.

6.5- Handling Payment Discrepancies: 
6.5.1- When a payment does not match the expected amount or claim record, the payment 
posting senior investigates the discrepancy. The process begins with reviewing the 
EOB, ERA, and supporting documents to determine the cause. If clarification is 
needed, the specialist contacts the payer or patient directly. Based on the findings, 
the account is adjusted to correct overpayments or underpayments, with each 
adjustment documented and justified. Unresolved issues are escalated to the team 
leader or department manager. All corrections and communications are recorded in 
the system and reflected in reconciliation reports, preserving revenue integrity and 
ensuring a full audit trail. 
6.6 Reporting and Documentation : 
6.6.1- At the end of each day and month, the Billing Specialist compiles all payment 
records and prepares required reports. Copies of EOBs, ERAs, and payment records 
are retained for reference and audit. Any identified discrepancies are documented 
with details of their resolution. Patient balance reports are updated to include 
adjustments and reconciliations. 
6.6.2- A Daily Payment Report (code: PS/B&A-F-28) summarizing all posted payments is 
submitted to the department manager. At month-end, a monthly report comparing 
posted payments with financial records is prepared for management review. These 
reports provide transparency, ensure financial accuracy, and confirm that all billing 
operations align with company standards.

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this procedure is to establish clear and standardized process for verifying 
insurance coverage and determining authorization requirements, collecting and submitting 
prior authorization requests accurately and on time, monitoring and managing pending, denied, 
or expired authorizations to prevent delays in patient care, and defining clear guidelines for 
placing patients on hold with appropriate follow-up actions to ensure timely resolution and 
payment. These processes aim to prevent claim denials, ensure timely treatment authorization, 
and maintain accurate patient and billing records. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure applies to authorization agents and senior specialists involved in insurance 
verification, prior authorization, submission and tracking of requests, denial management and 
escalation, as well as placing patients on hold and ensuring proper follow-up and clearance. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology:

4.1- Authorization / Medical Necessity / Medical Review: Approval obtained from an 
insurance provider confirming coverage of the prescribed treatments. 
4.2- Initial Evaluation: The first session conducted by a physical therapist to assess the 
patient’s condition. 
4.3- Plan of Care (POC): A document outlining recommended treatments, frequency, and 
duration. 
4.4- Patient Filtration: A daily review to ensure patients who attended the clinic have valid 
authorizations. 
4.5- EMR System: Electronic system used for scheduling, clinical documentation, and patient 
records. 
4.6- ICD-10 Code: Standardized codes for diagnoses and medical conditions. 
4.7- CPT Code: Codes describing medical procedures and services. 
4.8- Modifier: Additional code providing more details about a service. 
4.9- Credentialing Information: Provider identification and verification details (NPI, tax ID, 
location). 
4.10- Medical Necessity Denial: Denial from insurance indicating lack of medical necessity. 
4.11- Referral Form: Document from a referring provider recommending treatment. 
4.12- Progress Notes: Clinical documentation recorded by the healthcare provider to track a 
patient’s condition, treatment, and progress over time. This includes initial notes and re-
evaluations, which are follow-up assessments used to review and update the patient’s 
status and response to treatment. 
4.13- Credentialing Issues: Problems related to a provider’s participation or network status. 
4.14- Peer-to-Peer Review: Discussion between provider and insurance reviewer to advocate 
for approval. 
4.15- Negative IME: An Independent Medical Examination (IME) conducted by the 
government for Workers’ Compensation (WC) or No-Fault (NF) patients to assess 
medical necessity. A negative IME indicates that the patient has reached Maximum 
Medical Improvement (MMI).

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Obtaining & Filtering Daily Patient Report: 
6.1.1- The authorization specialist retrieves yesterday’s patient list from the EMR and 
documents it in the workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21).  
6.1.2- Filters the list to identify patients needing new authorizations. 
6.1.3- Adds required cases only to workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). 
Output: Updated list of required authorization cases. 
Communication: Sheet shared with authorization, submission teams, client relations 
and collection. 
6.2- Submission of Daily Authorization Requests: 
6.2.1- Specialist prepares authorization requests from the cases entered in the workload sheet 
(code: PS/B&A-F-21). 
6.2.2- Submits via payer portal, fax, or phone, attaching clinical documents and referral if 
needed.  
6.2.3- Records confirmation numbers and dates in the workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). 
Output: Completed submissions with confirmation details. 
Communication: Updates visible in shared sheet with authorization, submission teams, 
client relations and collection. 
6.3- Pending Authorizations Review: 
6.3.1- Every two days, specialist reviews all “pending” cases. 
6.3.2- Follows up with payers for cases exceeding expected timelines. 
6.3.2- Updates the workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). 
Output: Updated pending authorization statuses. 
Communication: Escalations sent to senior specialist if needed. 
6.4- Denial Management & Investigations: 
6.4.1- Upon receiving a denial, specialist reviews the denial reason.  
6.4.2- Coordinates with providers or the auditor to gather missing details. 
6.4.3- Escalates complex cases to senior specialist. 
6.4.4- Records findings and actions in the workload sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-21). 
Output: Documented reason and next action. 
Communication: Updates shared via authorization sheet and to the authorization 
senior.

6.5- Incomplete Notes & Complicated Case Handling: 
6.5.1- Senior updates the incomplete notes daily (code: PS/B&A-F-29). 
6.5.2- Sends un-finalized claims to medical auditing to prevent delays. 
6.5.3- Manages complex documentation or denial issues requiring deeper follow-up. 
Output: Updated incomplete notes list and resolved gaps. 
Communication: Auditing team & authorization team notified. 
6.6- Hold Categories & Clearance Procedures: 
This part explains cases when putting the patient on hold is necessary and the applied 
protocol in each scenario: 
6.6.1- Referral Issues: 
Hold applied when referral is missing, incorrect, or unsigned. 
Output: Referral hold entry. 
Communication: Referral coordinators team receive these data and start working on it. 
6.6.2- Pending Authorization Requests: 
Holds reviewed when insurance pending exceeds 14 days, or MD does not respond after 
14 days. 
Output: Hold with follow-up date. 
Communication: MD/payer contacted for update. 
6.6.3- Re-Evaluation Rules: 
- 10+ visits approved → Proceed normal. 
- 6-9 visits approved → Once a week (except Healthfirst). 
- Healthfirst: 4th request once a week; 5th+ request → Hold. 
- <5 visits approved → Hold until further approval. 
- Non-MTG <9 visits → Once a week. 
Output: Updated frequency or hold applied. 
Communication: PT and the front desks notified through EMR and the Hold System.

6.6.4- Coordination of Benefits (COB): 
Hold applied when primary insurance is inactive or conflicting. 
Output: COB hold documented. 
Communication: Patient contacted for updated with the insurance. 
6.6.5- New Case Required: 
New case requested when authorization is denied with no further steps. 
Output: Opening new case; stop using the old case. 
Communication: Provider & front desk notified. 
6.6.6- Insurance Consent: 
Hold applied until required consent forms are received. 
Output: Consent hold entry. 
Communication: Front desk contacts patient. 
6.6.7- Discharge Letter: 
Hold applied until prior PT’s discharge letter is uploaded. 
Output: Cleared once received. 
Communication: Patients and front desks. 
6.6.8- Coverage Issues: 
Hold applied for terminated coverage or non-covered services. 
Output: Coverage hold documented. 
Communication: Patient contacted for new insurance or self-pay. 
6.6.9- Negative IME: 
Hold applied if IME indicates no medical necessity. 
Output: IME hold entry. 
Communication: Patient informed.

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this procedure is to establish clear, consistent guidelines for the client relations 
team in handling patient and client inquiries, verifying insurance, managing documentation, 
and coordinating with other departments. It ensures that all processes are carried out in a 
professional, accurate, and timely manner to maintain compliance, support operational 
efficiency, and enhance overall patient and client satisfaction. 
2- Scope: 
The scope of this procedure includes verifying insurance coverage for patients, handling 
workers’ compensation and no-fault cases, managing inbound and outbound calls for benefits 
and eligibility verification, maintaining documentation and records for revenue cycle inquiries, 
managing payments and copays, coordinating with other departments for inquiries received via 
Google Spaces or calls by forwarding concerns related to billing, authorizations, medical 
records, or clinical matters with all necessary patient details, and facilitating communication 
with clients and patients. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015.

4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Client/Patient: 
Refers to the individual receiving physical therapy services or inquiring about services. 
4.2- Eligibility: 
The process of verifying if the patient’s insurance plan is active and covers physical 
therapy services. 
4.3- Benefits Verification: 
The process of confirming specific insurance benefits, such as copay, deductible, and visit 
limits. 
4.5- Self-Pay : 
Patients who pay out-of-pocket. Before insurance begins to cover visits or who does not 
have insurance to cover 
4.6-Bundle: 
A pre-paid package of visits for self-pay patients. 
4.7- Copay : 
A fixed amount paid by the patient at each visit, as required by their insurance 
4.8- Deductible 
The amount a patient must pay out-of-pocket before insurance begins to cover services. 
4.9- Coinsurance : 
A percentage of the service cost that the patient pays after the deductible is met. 
4.10-WC Case (Workers’ Compensation) : 
A case related to workplace injuries, covered by employer’s insurance. 
4.11- NF Case (No-Fault) : 
A case related to auto accidents, covered by no-fault insurance. 
4.12- Client Relations Sheet: 
The team’s internal document used to track eligibility, calls, follow-ups, and case updates. 
4.13-Google Spaces: 
The primary communication platform used by the team to coordinate tasks and share 
updates. 
4.14- Front Desk 
Clinic-based staff responsible for scheduling, collecting payments, and patient check-ins.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Insurance Verification Process: 
6.1.1- Receive the Concern: 
The agent reviews the concern received through Google Spaces. 
6.1.2- Access Patient Chart: 
● Open the patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11) in WebPT. 
● Retrieve the insurance card from the eDocs section. 
6.1.3- Identify & Verify Insurance: 
● Identify the insurance provider. 
● Log in to the appropriate insurance portal. 
● Verify the patient’s eligibility and benefits specifically related to physical therapy 
services. 
6.1.4- Confirm Financial Responsibilities: 
● Determine whether the patient has any copayments, deductibles, or coinsurance. 
6.1.5- Update Patient Record. 
● Update the patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11) in WebPT/EMR system with the 
verified insurance information. 
Output: 
● Verified patient insurance eligibility and benefit confirmation. 
● Information is communicated with the Front Desk, Customer Care, and the Client via 
Google Spaces. 
Reasoning: 
● To ensure patient satisfaction by validating and confirming their insurance information 
accurately. 
6.2-Authorization Handling 
6.2.1- Receive Authorization Status: 
● The agent receives authorization status calls from insurance companies. 
● Verify whether the authorization is approved, denied, or not yet received.

6.2.2- Record Authorization Details: 
● Document all relevant details in the authorization details form (code: PS/B&A-F-33), 
including: 
o Approval number 
o Validity period 
o Number of visits approved 
6.2.3- Forward to Authorization Agent 
● Share the recorded information with the authorization agent via Google Spaces for 
further handling. 
Output: 
● Timely follow-up and proper handling by the authorization agent. 
Reasoning: 
● To maintain clear, accurate records of patient authorizations. 
● To avoid delays in patient care and ensure compliance with insurance requirements. 
6.3- Handling Special Cases (WC/NF): 
6.3.1- Verify Coverage: 
● The Client Relations Agent contacts the insurance company and/or adjuster directly to 
confirm Workers’ Compensation (WC) or No-Fault (NF) coverage. 
6.3.2- Document Communication: 
● Record all communication attempts (calls, emails, or faxes) with detailed notes in the 
client relation sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-33). 
● Ensure confirmation is obtained before scheduling or proceeding with patient visits. 
6.3.3- Track Case Status: 
● Maintain updates in the WC/NF log. 
● Follow up regularly until full verification is complete. 
6.3.4- Communicate with Team 
● Update the front desk and care team regarding the case status. 
● Share confirmation of claim status and established body part via Google Spaces.

6.4- Handling billing and medical records inquiries 
6.4.1- Receive Inquiry:

billing charges, payments, or medical records. 
6.4.2- Verify Information: 
● Review and confirm details using WebPT, eDocs, and internal logs to ensure accuracy. 
6.4.3- Provide Clarification: 
● Communicate accurate and clear information back to the patient or internal team. 
6.4.4- Escalate if Necessary: 
● Forward complex or unresolved cases to the Senior Client Relations Agent or Billing 
Department for further handling. 
Output: 
● Accurate and timely resolution of billing and medical records inquiries. 
Reasoning: 
● To maintain patient trust and satisfaction by providing clear and reliable information. 
● To ensure accurate coordination between client relations, billing, and internal teams. 
6.5- Monthly Eligibility Sheet Form (code: PS/B&A-F-30)   
6.5.1- Distribution: 
● The senior client relations agent distributes the monthly eligibility sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-30) at the start of each month.
