6.5.2- Assignment: 
● Each agent is assigned specific patients/insurances, indicated by their name on the sheet 
tab. 
6.5.3- Verification Steps: 
● Open the patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11) in WebPT. 
● Retrieve insurance details from the eDocs section (insurance card, policy number, etc.). 
● Access the insurance portal or call the payer directly. 
● Confirm if the patient’s insurance is active for physical therapy services. 
6.5.4- Documentation: 
● Mark “Y” (Yes) if insurance is active, or “N” (No) if inactive, using the agreed 
formatting and color-coding. 
● If a patient’s insurance is inactive: 
o Notify the front desk/customer care team immediately. 
o Add the patient to the hold system (code: PS/B&A-F-20) until updated information 
is received. 
6.5.5- Corrections & Updates: 
● Ensure any corrections (e.g., plan updates or resubmitted information) are re-verified 
and updated in the sheet promptly. 
6.5.6- Deadline Compliance: 
● Completion of the sheet must be finalized by the 10th of each month. 
Output: 
● Verified and updated patient eligibility status for all assigned patients. 
Reasoning: 
● To ensure accurate insurance verification and avoid scheduling errors or claim denials. 
● To maintain a clear workflow between Client Relations, Front Desk, and Customer 
Care teams.

6.6- Missing Documents Sheets Form: 
6.6.1- Generation & Distribution: 
● The missing documents sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-31) is generated and shared regularly 
with the client relations team. 
6.6.2- Assignment: 
● Each agent is assigned a portion of clinics to review. 
6.6.3- Verification Steps: 
● Open the patient’s chart (code: PS/B&A-F-11) in WebPT. 
● Cross-check the chart against the missing documents listed (e.g., Insurance Card, 
National ID). 
6.6.4- Documentation Handling: 
● If the required document is available: 
o Update the sheet with the Date of Service and mark as “No Missing.” 
● If the document is still missing: 
o Record it in the sheet for the Front Desk team to follow up on. 
o The Front Desk is responsible for contacting the patient and uploading the missing 
document into the system. 
Output: 
● Accurate tracking of missing or completed patient documents. 
Reasoning: 
● To ensure all patient documentation is complete and updated in WebPT. 
● To facilitate smooth claims processing, compliance, and accurate patient records 
6.7- Missing Copay and No-Show Fees Form (code: PS/B&A-F-32) 
6.7.1- Generate Weekly Report in WebPT: 
● Access the Analytics section in the WebPT system. 
● Open the Patient Payments tab and select the date range for the past week. 
● Generate a report of all copays and no-show fees to identify payments that have not 
been collected.

1- Purpose: 
The purpose of this document is to provide a clear and detailed procedure for submitting 
medical claims to insurance companies. It outlines the steps necessary to ensure accurate and 
timely submission of claims. 
2- Scope: 
This document is intended for administrative and billing staff, and insurance claim processors 
who are responsible for submitting medical claims on behalf of the practice or facility. It covers 
the entire process of claim submission, from gathering patient information to submitting claims 
through the appropriate channels (WebPT- Billing, Claim MD, etc.) and ensuring that all 
required modifiers, documentation, and authorizations are included. The procedure applies to 
all types of claims, including commercial insurance, Workers' Compensation (WC), and No-
Fault (NF) claims. 
3- References: 
3.1- HIPAA (Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act) — Compliance for secure 
handling of patient health and billing information. 
3.2- Payer-Specific billing guidelines, including Medicare guidelines, Medicaid, and 
commercial insurance policies.

Page 3 of 7 
3.3- CMS (Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services) Guidelines — For claims submission 
requirements, billing codes, and modifier usage. 
3.4- CPT (Current Procedural Terminology) and ICD-10 Coding Manuals — For accurate 
coding of procedures and diagnoses. 
3.5- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Claim: 
A request for payment submitted to an insurance payer for services provided to a patient. 
4.2- Primary Insurance:  
The patient’s main insurance payer that is billed first. 
4.3- Secondary Insurance:  
The insurance payer billed after the primary has processed the claim (if applicable). 
4.4- Payer:  
The insurance company or organization responsible for reimbursing medical claims. 
4.5- Clearinghouse:  
A third-party service that reviews and transmits electronic claims between providers and 
payers. 
4.6- E-Submission:  
Electronic submission of claims through the billing software or clearinghouse. 
4.7- Timely Filing Limit:  
The maximum time frame allowed by the payer to accept a claim after the date of service. 
4.8- EOB (Explanation of Benefits):  
A statement from the insurance payer explaining how a claim was processed and paid. 
4.9- Resubmission:  
Submitting a corrected claim after a rejection or denial.  
4.10- Submission Sheet:  
The internal document or tracker used to record claim submission details for follow-up 
and audit purposes.

4.11- Wept Billing / Claim MD:  
The software platforms used for claim creation, submission, and reconciliation. 
4.12- Batch Submission:  
Submitting multiple claims at once to the clearinghouse or payer. 
4.13- Claim Status:  
The current state of a claim in the submission and payment cycle (e.g., Submitted, 
Rejected, Denied, Paid). 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation: 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
Responsible for ensuring the processes described in this procedure 
achieve their intended outcomes. 
Responsible for ensuring that the procedure requirements are 
integrated within business processes. 
Agents (Submission 
/ Billing Specialists) 
Review all pended claims daily and ensure the accuracy and 
completeness of patient demographics, insurance, and claim-
related information before submission. Perform thorough claim 
scrubbing to identify and correct any missing, inaccurate, or 
invalid data in accordance with internal checklists and compliance 
standards. Submit claims manually through WebPT Billing when 
electronic submission is not possible, ensuring adherence to 
defined submission timelines and confirming successful 
submission. 
Monitor 
daily 
rejection 
reports 
from 
the 
clearinghouse, identify root causes, correct errors promptly, 
resubmit claims without delay, and document all actions and 
updates to support timely and accurate claims processing. 
Credentialing 
Department 
Ensures that the provider and location are appropriate for the 
specific insurance plan being submitted. They also handle the 
enrollment of each provider with the relevant insurance 
companies to ensure eligibility and proper claim submission.

documentation, including progress notes. They are responsible for

Billing / 
Submission Senior  
Coordinate daily schedules and claim distribution, monitor 
incomplete notes in collaboration with the medical auditing team, 
and supervise agents to ensure timely and accurate claim 
submission. Track daily submissions through clearinghouse 
reports, maintain organized records, and perform reconciliation 
between scheduled visits and submitted claims to ensure 
completeness, accuracy, and compliance. 
 
6- Procedures: 
6.1- Daily Schedule Review & Task Preparation: 
6.1.1- The submission senior extracts yesterday’s finalized schedule from the submission 
tool sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-34). 
6.1.2- Filters the list to include finalized visits only. 
6.1.3- Adds all patients and visit details to the daily submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-
35). 
6.1.4- Assigns each case to the responsible submission agent according to location. 
Output: Updated daily submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-35) with assigned cases. 
Communication: Sheet accessible to submission team, quality team, authorization, 
and collections. 
6.2- Incomplete Notes Management: 
6.2.1- The submission senior reviews the incomplete/missing notes report twice daily (code: 
PS/B&A-F-36). 
6.2.2- Updates the incomplete notes tracking sheet with all missing documentation. 
6.2.3- Sends the list of incomplete notes to the medical auditing team in notes & appt type 
audit sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-37). 
6.2.4- Updates daily submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-35) once notes are finalized. 
Output: Updated list of incomplete/finalized notes. 
Communication: Shared tracker updated for medical auditing, submission team, and 
clinical coordinators team.

6.3- Claim Review & Scrubbing: 
6.3.1- Submission agents review pended claims and correct demographics, insurance, and 
authorization details in charge audit / charge review on WebPT (code: PS/B&A-F-
38). 
6.3.2- Scrub claims by verifying coding, modifiers, units, and payer requirements. 
6.3.3- Mark claims as “Invoiced” in daily submission sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-35). 
Output: Scrubbed claims prepared for electronic or manual submission. 
Communication: Updated status visible to seniors and team leaders. 
6.4- Manual Submission & Rejection Handling: 
6.4.1- Submission agent manually submits claims that cannot be submitted electronically 
(WC, NF, select commercial). 
6.4.2- Reviews rejected claims in clearinghouse (code: PS/B&A-F-39), applies corrections, 
and resubmits  
6.4.3- Attaches medical records when required by payer. 
Output: Corrected and successfully resubmitted claims. 
Communication: Rejection log and updates shared with seniors and team leaders. 
6.5- Daily Reconciliation & Reporting: 
6.5.1- Submission senior exports the daily submission report from the clearinghouse. 
6.5.2- Reconciles submitted claims against the daily submission sheet and scheduled visits. 
6.5.3- Ensures all cases assigned for the day have been submitted or noted. 
Output: Daily reconciliation summary. 
Communication: Report shared with team leader, clinical supervisors, finance 
department in submission report (code: PS/B&A-F-40).

1- Purpose: 
1.1- The purpose of this procedure is to ensure accurate and timely billing for DOE pediatric 
services, proper invoicing, and submission for reimbursement. 
1.2- This procedure aims to streamline the process for handling DOE pediatric procedures, 
ensuring accurate billing and timely payments, and serve as a guideline for the Review 
process carried out by QA department. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure is intended for all personnel involved in the processing of DOE billing for 
pediatric patients. 
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- DOE: 
Department of Education – The government agency responsible for overseeing and 
ensuring that educational programs and services comply with state and federal regulations. 
In the context of pediatric services, DOE may refer to the department that governs the 
standards for educational services provided to students with disabilities.

4.2- EMR (Electronic Medical Record):   
A digital version of a patient's medical chart, containing important health information, 
appointment details, and therapy documentation, used for scheduling, patient care, and 
billing purposes. 
4.3- Vendor Portal (vPortal): 
The Department of Education’s online management tool for vendors. The vPortal is used 
for submitting and managing electronic invoices, tracking payments, and handling all 
vendor-related activities in the billing process. It provides a secure, organized platform for 
vendors to interact with the Department of Education to ensure timely reimbursement. 
4.4- RSA: Risk Services Agreement: 
A formal document that outlines the terms and conditions of a healthcare service 
agreement, including responsibilities, liabilities, and expectations. 
4.5- Bid: 
A proposal for services and costs created by the clinic. After the agreement is signed and 
submitted, the clinic owner must approve the bid before billing can begin. 
4.6- Providers: 
Healthcare professionals (e.g., therapists, doctors) who provide DOE services to children, 
such as physical therapy, speech therapy, or occupational therapy, and are responsible for 
documenting patient progress and needs. 
4.7- Service Coordinator: 
A key role in managing and coordinating patient-related information. The service 
coordinator ensures that all necessary agreements and documents, such as the RSA, are 
received, verified, and submitted for district approval. They also assist with 
communication between the billing team and other departments to resolve issues. 
4.8- Billing Specialist: 
A staff member who reviews, processes, and ensures the accuracy of billing 
documentation, including EI service notes, before submitting them for payment. 
4.9- Billing Manager: 
The person in charge of overseeing the billing team, ensuring compliance with billing 
procedures, and managing the overall billing process for DOE services within the 
organization.

4.10- Finance Department: 
The department responsible for managing the financial aspects of the organization, 
including tracking payments, addressing billing discrepancies, and following up on 
outstanding DOE services payments. 
4.11- Front Office: 
The administrative team is responsible for managing patient appointments, handling 
patient communications, and ensuring that invoices are prepared for billing submission. 
4.12- Front Desk Manager: 
The person overseeing the front office team, ensuring proper appointment scheduling and 
smooth operations for patient registration and communication. 
4.13- Clinic Doctor Owner: 
The physician or clinic owner who oversees the operation of the clinic, ensuring that all 
procedures are following regulations and organizational policies. 
4.14- Quality Assurance: 
Quality Assurance (QA) ensures that services, processes, and documentation meet 
established standards.  
4.15- District Office Representative: 
A role within the district office that handles the review and approval of agreements and 
contracts related to patient care. The District Office Representative ensures that all 
agreements are in place before a patient is entered into the system for billing purposes. 
They also verify the accuracy of the information before it is sent to the provider. 
4.16- SESIS: 
Special Education Student Information System

the provider’s treatment. 
Accountable for ensuring proper training and support for all clinic 
staff involved in DOE services. 
 
6- Procedures: 
6.1- Agreement Signing (Provider): 
6.1.1- The district office sends the service agreement form (code: PS/B&A-F-41) to the 
service coordinator, who communicates it to the provider. 
6.1.2- The provider reviews the agreement with the patient or guardian and signs the service 
agreement form (code: PS/B&A-F-41) 
6.1.3- After both signatures are obtained, the provider returns the agreement to the service 
coordinator, who submits it to the district office. 
6.2- Agreement Submission (Service Coordinator): 
6.2.1- The service coordinator submits the signed agreement to the district office for 
approval. 
6.2.2- The district office approves the agreement by issuing an agreement approval letter 
(code: PS/B&A-F-42). The patient is then entered into the SESIS portal, visible on 
the SESIS Patient (code: PS/B&A-F-43).

6.3- Bid Acceptance (Clinic Doctor/Owner): 
6.3.1- Once the patient is entered into the SESIS portal, the district office adds the patient 
to the bid list in SESIS Patient (code: PS/B&A-F-43) for provider review. The clinic 
doctor reviews and accepts the bid in the SESIS portal bid acceptance screen. 
6.3.2- After acceptance, the bid is closed and awarded, visible on the SESIS bid awarded 
clearing the patient for appointment scheduling. 
6.4- Appointment Scheduling (Front Office): 
6.4.1- Following bid approval, the front office schedules the patient’s appointment and 
records the details in the WebPT schedule (code: PS/B&A-F-04). 
6.4.2- The billing specialist retrieves the appointment details for billing purposes in the DOE 
WebPT Sheet (code: PS/B&A-F-44). 
6.5- Patient Visit & Paper Invoice (Provider & Front Desk): 
6.5.1- The provider meets with the patient and documents all services provided. 
6.5.2- The provider sends the completed paper invoice (code: PS/B&A-F-45) to the front 
desk, which uploads it to the designated folder in google drive  for the billing 
specialist. 
6.6- Invoice Review & Entry: 
6.6.1- The billing specialist reviews the paper invoice in the drive for accuracy. 
6.6.2- Once verified, the billing specialist enters the invoice details into the Vendor Portal 
(vPortal). This process involves navigating the vPortal interface  selecting the 
appropriate invoice type and associating the services with the correct student profile 
in vPortal data entry (code: PS/B&A-F-46) before final submission. 
6.7- Billing Issues: 
6.7.1- After entering the sessions, the billing specialist identifies and reports any issues using 
the billing issue reporting form (code: PS/B&A-F-47) to the front office or service 
coordinator. 
6.7.2- The service coordinator or front desk provides the corrected information to the billing 
specialist for revision.

7. The Provider/Payee must obtain security clearance from the DOE, including background and fingerprint checks, 
for the Provider before service may begin. The Payee must also obtain such clearance for all personnel with access 
to the student’s records before such access is granted. The Payee must maintain all clearance(s) for the full duration 
of service provision and/or access to student records. 
8. The Provider/Payee must ensure that the Provider’s certification/license, registration, and security clearance 
remain current for the full duration of service provision. 
9. The Provider must inform the DOE of the initiation and termination dates of service via the Interactive Voice 
Response (IVR) system, or other system specified by the DOE. 
10. The Provider/Payee will keep records necessary to disclose the extent and nature of services the Provider 
furnishes to the student, including contemporaneously submitting Encounter Attendance and Session Notes via 
the SESIS system, or another mechanism specified by the DOE, at no additional cost to the DOE. Such attendance 
entries and notes must be provided for all sessions and contain all information required by the DOE. 
11. Payee must maintain and produce all records that support service provision and billing for audit or other review, 
including provider/employee/consultant time and payroll records, and must cooperate with all government 
investigations. Records must be retained for six years from the date of RSA-2 approval by the DOE, the date the 
record was created, or the date of transmission or entry into DOE systems, whichever is latest, or longer if 
specifically required by the DOE. 
12. The Payee will ensure that the Provider prepares and submits Student Progress Reports upon request, attend IEP 
conferences as required, and complete the appropriate pages of the student’s IEPs at no cost to the DOE. 
13. By signing this agreement, the Provider/Payee fully accepts all New York State (NYS) and DOE Medicaid 
requirements including the terms of the NYS Provider Agreement and the NYS Statement of Reassignment. The 
Provider agrees to remit all Medicaid reimbursements to the DOE, and not bill Medicaid directly for any services 
that the DOE will bill for under the school health program. Upon request, the Provider/Payee agrees to furnish 
the NYS Department of Health or its designee, the US Department of Health and Human Services, and the NYS 
Medicaid Fraud Control Unit with requested information on the services provided to the Student. The 
Provider/Payee agrees to comply with Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Section 504 of the Federal 
Rehabilitation Act, and all other State and Federal statutory and constitutional non-discrimination provisions, and 
to abide by all applicable Federal and State laws and regulations, including the Social Security Act, the New York 
Social Services Law, Part 42 of the C.F.R., and Title 18 of the NYCRR. The Provider/Payee must be familiar with 
and adhere to the DOE’s Medicaid Compliance Plan (“the Plan”). The Provider/Payee must immediately notify 
the DOE if the Provider is excluded from any Federal health care program or NYS Medicaid program. 
14. The Provider must have a National Provider Identifier (NPI) number, which can be obtained at the NPPES 
website. That number must be entered in the appropriate field of each electronic invoice. 
15. Payment shall be made at the applicable RSA rate established by the DOE for the discipline, district/borough, 
and language. Such rate shall be paid solely for time spent providing direct services and shall not vary with the 
size of the group served, except where specifically and explicitly indicated on the RSA schedule. If Payee is also 
under separate contract with the DOE to provide the same discipline, district/borough and service, payment for 
services under this Agreement will be paid in accordance with the rates stated in such separate contract. Invoices 
pursuant to this Agreement must be submitted electronically on a monthly basis through a system designated by

submitted later than 90 days after the end of the month in which services were provided. 
16. The Provider/Payee will protect and not disclose to third parties any student data or other confidential 
information, unless required by law or court order, and will comply with the Family Educational Rights and 
Privacy Act, and Chancellor’s Regulation A-820, governing access to and disclosure of student information. 
17. The Provider/Payee will adhere to New York City Charter Chapter 68 Conflict of Interest Rules. Neither the 
Provider nor the Payee may be the student’s relative or relative of Payee. Provider and Payee are prohibited from 
giving gifts to DOE staff, parents, or students regardless of value. 
18. Pursuant to the New York City Conflict of Interest Board (NYC COIB) Rules, a current full-time employee of 
the DOE cannot provide services under this Agreement unless the services are covered by the mass waivers 
granted by the NYC COIB, or an individual waiver is granted by the NYC COIB based on a request from the 
DOE. Part-time DOE employees need individual waivers. Individuals who leave the employ of the DOE, 
including those performing per diem or part-time work, may not provide services under this Agreement for one 
full year after leaving DOE employment unless they obtain an individual waiver from the NYC COIB. 
19. If the Payee’s aggregate business with the DOE during any twelve-month period is projected by the DOE to 
exceed $100,000, it must complete VENDEX questionnaires, submit them to the NYC Mayor’s Office of Contract 
Services, and enter into a standard contract with the DOE. The Payee agrees that it is not entitled to payment 
during any twelve-month period in excess of $100,000 without entering into a contract with the DOE. 
20. Without prior approval from the DOE Representative, Provider or Payee may not provide Related Services to the 
Student if the Provider/Payee had previously performed a related services evaluation or assessment of the Student. 
21. The Provider/Payee must carry professional malpractice/liability insurance for the services provided under this 
Agreement. 
22. If the Provider/Payee fails to adhere fully to any of the above-stated conditions 7 through 21, the DOE reserves 
the right to withhold payment for Services. The DOE reserves the right to withhold payment if there is any dispute 
with the Provider, the Payee, or any entity affiliated with the Provider and/or Payee. 
23. The DOE may terminate this Agreement upon 10 days’ prior written notice to the Parent/Guardian and the 
Provider/Payee, or immediately for cause.

1- Purpose: 
This procedure defines the process for billing and authorization for early intervention pediatric 
care. 
2- Scope: 
This procedure governs all personnel involved in the processing of early intervention (EI) notes 
for pediatric patients.  
3- References: 
3.1- The International Standard ISO 9001:2015. 
4- Terms / Terminology:  
4.1- Early intervention: 
A service provided to children with developmental delays or health problems, including 
therapies such as physical therapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy, designed to 
address issues early in a child’s development. 
4.2- EMR (Electronic Medical Record):   
A digital version of a patient's medical chart, containing important health information, 
appointment details, and therapy documentation, used for scheduling, patient care, and 
billing purposes.

Page 3 of 8 
4.3- Challenge Portal: 
An online platform used for submitting completed Early Intervention notes, billing 
documents, and related materials for reimbursement processing. 
4.4- Billing Sheets (Challenge Sheets): 
A tool used for billing each Early Intervention session, containing patient EI IDs and 
session details. The sheet helps calculate the amount due per session and tracks patient 
visits for accurate billing. 
4.5- Cancellation Notes: 
Notes that document any patient appointment cancellations, including the reason for 
cancellation and relevant dates, submitted as part of the billing documentation for correct 
processing and reimbursement. 
4.6- Providers: 
Healthcare professionals (e.g., therapists, doctors) who provide early intervention services 
to children, such as physical therapy, speech therapy, or occupational therapy, and are 
responsible for documenting patient progress and needs. 
4.7- Service Coordinator: 
The person responsible for managing and coordinating the care for the child, combining 
the therapy notes with signature logs, and submitting the documentation to the Billing 
Specialist for review 
4.8- Billing Specialist: 
A staff member who reviews, processes, and ensures the accuracy of billing 
documentation, including EI service notes, before submitting them for payment. 
4.9- Billing Manager: 
The person in charge of overseeing the billing team, ensuring compliance with billing 
procedures, and managing the overall billing process for EI services within the 
organization. 
4.10- Finance Department: 
The department is responsible for managing the financial aspects of the organization, 
including tracking payments, addressing billing discrepancies, and following up on 
outstanding payments for EI services.

4.11- Front Desk: 
The administrative team responsible for scheduling appointments, managing patient 
records in the EMR system, and facilitating communication between patients and 
healthcare providers. 
4.12- Front Desk Manager:  
The person overseeing the front desk team, ensuring proper appointment scheduling and 
smooth operations for patient registration and communication. 
4.13- Clinic Doctor Owner:  
The physician or clinic owner who oversees the operation of the clinic, ensuring that all 
procedures are compliant with regulations and organizational policies.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Authorization Receipt: 
6.1.1- The service coordinator receives patient authorization from the early intervention 
center (Challenge), referred to as the challenge authorization form (code: PS/B&A-
F-50). 
6.1.2- The authorization includes the approved specialty or specialties and the mandate, 
specifying the number of sessions per week and session duration (details are found 
on the challenge authorization form (code: PS/B&A-F-50). 
6.1.3- Upon receiving the authorization, the service coordinator informs the front desk to 
begin scheduling appointments. 
6.2- Appointment Scheduling: 
6.2.1- The front desk schedules the patient’s appointment, recording it in the WebPT Clinic 
Schedule (code: PS/B&A-F-04). 
6.2.2- The billing specialist extracts relevant appointment details for billing purposes.

6.3- Patient Visit & Paper Notes Filling (Provider): 
6.3.1- The provider conducts the therapy session with the patient. 
6.3.2- The provider completes the corresponding paper session note form (code: PS/B&A-
F-51) documenting the services provided during the session. 
6.3.3- The provider submits the completed notes to the service coordinator. 
6.4- Combining Documentation (Service Coordinator): 
6.4.1- The service coordinator combines the paper session note form (code: PS/B&A-F-51) 
and the signature log form (code: PS/B&A-F-52), recording dates of attendance and 
CPT codes for each session. 
6.4.2- The combined documentation is forwarded to the billing specialist. 
6.5- Reviewing Notes & Billing (Billing Specialist): 
6.5.1- Once the notes are verified, the billing specialist begins the billing process. 
6.5.2- The billing specialist reviews the notes and cross-checks them with the sheet (code: 
PS/B&A-F-45) for discrepancies or missing information. 
6.6- Reporting Issues (Billing Specialist): 
6.6.1- If any issues are discovered, the billing specialist informs the provider using the issue 
reporting form (code: PS/B&A-F-47). 
6.7- Correcting Issues: 
6.7.1- The provider addresses the issues raised by the billing specialist, makes the necessary 
corrections, and returns the updated notes via the service coordinator. 
6.8- Documentation Completion: 
6.8.1- The billing specialist finalizes the documentation after corrections are made. 
6.8.2- This includes verifying all notes, finalizing cancellation note forms (PS/B&A-F-53), 
and completing the challenge billing sheet (PS/B&A-F-54). 
6.9- Submission to Challenge Portal: 
6.9.1- The billing specialist compiles all required documentation (including notes, challenge 
billing sheet (PS/B&A-F-54), and cancellation note forms (PS/B&A-F-53), and 
submits them through the "Upload" tab on the challenge portal (code: PS/B&A-F-
55) for processing and reimbursement.

6.10.2- If payment is delayed, the billing manager follows up and then communicates with

1- Purpose: 
This procedure establishes a structured process to ensure that all projects are effectively 
monitored, tracked, and aligned with organizational objectives, ISO requirements, and client 
expectations. It defines project follow-up, communication (including Voice Ticketing 
System), risk and change management, and reporting. 
2- Scope: 
2.1- Applies to all departments participating in project execution, monitoring, and reporting, 
including PMO, Operations, QA, Finance, IT, and other functional teams. 
2.2- Covers the entire project lifecycle, from initiation to closure, for internal projects, client 
projects, and externally funded initiatives. 
3- References 
3.1- ISO 9001:2015 
3.2- Communication Plan (PS/TM-F-08) 
3.3- Document Control Procedure (PS/QA-P-01)

4- Terms / Terminology 
4.1- Project Manager (PM): Individual responsible for project execution, milestones, and 
deliverables. 
4.2- Project Management Team (PMT): Cross-functional oversight team for monitoring and 
coordination. 
4.3- Project Follow-Up Form (PS/PM-F-01): Document for tracking milestones, deliverables, 
responsibilities, and status. 
4.4- Project Review Minutes (PS/PM-F-02): Template for recording meetings, decisions, and 
action items. 
4.5- Risk and Issue Register (PS/PM-F-03): Document for tracking project-specific risks and 
issues. 
4.6- Voice Ticketing System (VTS): Platform for logging voice updates, feedback, and 
escalations. 
4.7- Project Charter: Defines scope, objectives, milestones, resources, and risks. 
 
5- Responsibility for Use and Preservation 
Title 
Responsibility 
CEO 
Ensure procedure achieves intended outcomes and aligns 
with organizational strategy. 
Head of Operations / PM Head 
Oversee communication plans, project follow-up, and 
approvals. 
Project Manager 
Execute project monitoring, update forms, track risks, and 
communicate via VTS. 
QA Manager 
Audit documentation, ensure ISO compliance. 
Project Team Members 
Provide updates, report risks/issues, support project progress.

6- Procedures: 
6.1- Project Suggestion, Prioritization, and Launch: 
- Projects proposed by Department Heads or Head of OPEX. 
- Prioritization via Project Priority Matrix by PM Head and Top Management. 
- PM assigned by PM Head; forms project team with departmental reps. 
- Project Charter prepared and approved by CEO.  
6.2- Project Follow-Up: 
- PM updates the steps of project weekly PS/PM-F-01. 
- Updates communicated via VTS to PM Head, QA, Project Team, and Top Management 
(for high-priority projects). It recorded in support request form code (PS/OpEx-F-01) 
- Records are retained 3 years. 
6.3- Progress Review Meetings: 
6.3.1 Meeting Structure: 
- PMT: Head of OPEX, PM Head, Project Manager, QA, Functional Leads, Top 
Management for strategic projects. 
* Frequency by priority:  
Priority 
Frequency 
High 
Weekly 
Medium 
Biweekly 
Low 
Monthly 
- PM schedules meetings attendees determined from Project Charter and 
Communication Plan. 
6.3.2 Meeting Minutes: 
- Recorded by Project Coordinator using PS/PM-F-01. 
- Distributed within 48 hours via VTS and email. 
- Action items logged in VTS, tracked to closure. 
6.4 Internal and External Reporting: 
6.4.1 Internal: 
- PM follow up with the Department Managers and reported any delays or 
communication issues to QA per Communication Plan.

6.4.2 External: 
- Submitted to clients or partners via E-mail. 
* Frequency by priority:  
Priority 
Frequency

Monthly 
- Coordinator confirms receipt; feedback logged in VTS. 
6.4.3 Forecasting: 
- Reports include summary, key risks, mitigation, and next period forecast. 
6.5 Risk and Issue Management: 
6.5.1 Recording: 
Each project maintains PS/PM-F-03 Risk & Issue Register. 
6.5.2 Review: 
- Risks reviewed in every PMT meeting; mitigation actions assigned based on the 
priority of each project. 
6.5.3 Escalation:  
- High-impact issues escalated via VTS to Top Management and per communication 
Plan. 
6.6 Change Control: 
6.6.1 Change Request: 
- Starting from the Top management regarding any change. 
- Any change documented as new project and recorded in Monthly Strategic Form 
code (PS/PM-F-01) 
- PM Team with the stakeholders work on action plan to procced. 
- PM Team Follow up on everything working smoothly.  
- PM completes PS/PM-F-01, reviewed by PM Head & QA, approved by CEO/Head 
of OPEX. 
6.6.2 Communication: 
- Changes reflected in Project Plan in Monthly Strategic Form code (PS/PM-F-01). 
- Stakeholders notified via VTS and email.

6.7 Archiving and Record Retention: 
6.7.1 Document Retention: 
- All project forms, reports, VTS logs retained 3 years per PS/QA-P-01.
6.7.2 Measuring Outcomes: 
- Compare project results vs. Project Charter objectives using KPIs:
- % Projects Completed on Time
- % Risk Mitigation
- Client Satisfaction ≥4/5
- % Project Effectiveness = (Actual ÷ Intended Outcomes) × 100
- It is tracked through Monthly Strategic Form code PS/PM-F-01
